intro.value: A short reference for where Taylor-series coefficients come from when a derivation expands a function around zero.
The key point is that the visible numeric coefficients are not arbitrary. They come from the Taylor formula itself and then combine with whatever integrals remain.

content.value: The symbol f of K asymptotic to h of K as K tends to K sub c does not mean the two expressions are exactly equal. It means they have the same leading behavior near that limit.
Formally, asymptotic equivalence means the ratio f of K over h of K tends to 1. So replacing one by the other changes only higher-order corrections, not the dominant scaling.
That is exactly the point of Eq. 14: it keeps the universal leading square-root behavior near the threshold, not the full exact formula away from threshold.
For a smooth function expanded around x equals 0, the quadratic term always comes with one-half times the second derivative.
So if the linear term vanishes, the first correction to the constant term is not f double-prime of 0 times x squared. It is one-half f double-prime of 0 times x squared.
In the Eq. 14 derivation, the expanded variable is z equals K r sine theta. So the quadratic correction brings down one-half times g double-prime of 0 times z squared.
After substituting z equals K r sine theta, the quadratic part contributes one-half g double-prime of 0 times K squared r squared sine squared theta.
That term is still inside the theta integral, so its coefficient must be multiplied by the remaining angular integral of cosine squared theta times sine squared theta.
